content:
# Handwashing Instruction Poster

**Tool ID:** T009-01
**Block:** B009 (Handwashing Protocol)
**Domain:** Food Safety & Hygiene (D01_food_safety)
**Level:** L1
**Format:** Guide
**Role:** Manager
**Time Required:** 10-15 minutes to read; permanent display
**Version:** 1.0

---
## Welcome

This is a foundational tool -- the kind that prevents problems before they start. It is designed to be picked up and used immediately, no training required.

A practical guide that gives your team the knowledge they need, in language that makes sense on a busy cafe floor. Proper handwashing is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of foodborne illness. This poster ensures every team member does it the same way, every time, at the right moments.

**Built for Manager working in Food Safety & Hygiene.**
Use it. Adapt it. Make it yours.

---

## Purpose

A4 laminated poster showing the correct 20-second handwashing technique step by step, positioned above every handwash basin in the cafe. Includes when to wash, how to wash, and common mistakes to avoid.

This tool supports **Handwashing Protocol** (B009) and is designed to be practical, printable, and immediately useful in a working cafe environment.

---

## How to Use This Tool

**Who:** Manager (prints and posts); All Staff (follows daily)
**Time Required:** 10-15 minutes to read and understand; then followed automatically
**Frequency:** Every time hands need washing (see trigger points below)

**What You'll Need:**
- This poster printed on A4, laminated, and posted above EVERY handwash basin
- Designated handwash basins (not the pot wash or food prep sink)
- Antibacterial hand soap (liquid, not bar soap)
- Paper towels or hand dryer (not shared cloth towels)
- Warm running water

**Steps:**
1. Print this poster on A4
2. Laminate it (condensation from sinks will destroy unlaminated paper)
3. Post it at eye level above every handwash basin in the cafe
4. Brief all staff during the next team meeting
5. Demonstrate the technique personally -- do not just point at the poster
6. Check supplies (soap, paper towels) are stocked at every basin every shift

---

## Guide

### When to Wash Your Hands (Trigger Points)

You MUST wash your hands at these moments, no exceptions:

| Trigger | Why |
|---------|-----|
| Before starting work / beginning a shift | Remove bacteria from commute, door handles, personal items |
| After using the toilet | Critical -- faecal bacteria cause the most serious foodborne illness |
| After handling raw food (meat, eggs, unwashed vegetables) | Prevent cross-contamination to ready-to-eat food |
| After touching your face, hair, nose, or mouth | Your skin carries Staphylococcus aureus -- a common food poisoning organism |
| After sneezing, coughing, or blowing your nose | Respiratory droplets carry bacteria and viruses |
| After handling waste or bins | Bins harbour high concentrations of bacteria |
| After handling cleaning chemicals | Prevent chemical contamination of food |
| After eating, drinking, or smoking during a break | Remove bacteria from personal food and cigarettes |
| After handling money (cash, cards) | Money is one of the most contaminated objects in daily life |
| Before handling ready-to-eat food (sandwiches, cakes, garnishes) | The last barrier between bacteria and the customer |
| When switching between tasks (e.g., clearing tables to making drinks) | Prevent transfer between contaminated and clean areas |
| After handling allergen-containing ingredients before preparing allergen-free items | Prevent allergen cross-contact |

### The 20-Second Handwashing Technique

#### Step 1: Wet (5 seconds)
- Turn on warm water (not cold -- soap works better with warm water)
- Wet hands thoroughly under running water
- Do NOT touch the taps again with clean hands -- use a paper towel to turn off

#### Step 2: Soap (2 seconds)
- Apply antibacterial liquid hand soap
- Enough to cover all surfaces of both hands

#### Step 3: Lather and Scrub (20 seconds minimum)
Follow this sequence to cover every surface:

1. **Palm to palm** -- rub hands together vigorously
2. **Back of hands** -- right palm over left dorsum, fingers interlaced, then swap
3. **Between fingers** -- fingers interlaced, palm to palm
4. **Backs of fingers** -- curl fingers and rub backs against opposite palm
5. **Thumbs** -- clasp each thumb in opposite hand and rotate
6. **Fingertips and nails** -- rub fingertips in opposite palm in circular motion
7. **Wrists** -- rub each wrist with opposite hand

**Timing tip:** Hum "Happy Birthday" twice in your head -- that is approximately 20 seconds.

#### Step 4: Rinse (5 seconds)
- Rinse thoroughly under clean running water
- Ensure all soap is removed
- Keep hands pointed downward so water runs off fingertips, not back up arms

#### Step 5: Dry (10 seconds)
- Dry hands completely with a single-use paper towel
- Do NOT use a shared cloth towel (bacteria breeding ground)
- Use the paper towel to turn off the tap (avoid recontamination)
- Dispose of the paper towel in the bin -- do not leave on the counter

### Common Mistakes

| Mistake | Why It Matters | Fix |
|---------|---------------|-----|
| Washing for less than 20 seconds | Not enough time to remove transient bacteria. Most people wash for 6 seconds. | Use the "Happy Birthday twice" timing method. |
| Skipping between the fingers | Bacteria accumulate in the warm, moist spaces between fingers | Follow the interlaced fingers step every time |
| Not drying hands properly | Wet hands transfer 1000x more bacteria than dry hands | Always dry completely with paper towel |
| Using the food prep sink | Handwash basins must be separate from food prep sinks. Using the wrong sink risks contaminating food areas | Designated handwash basins only -- label them clearly |
| Wearing rings and watches | Bacteria hide under jewellery where soap cannot reach | Remove rings, watches, and bracelets before washing. Plain wedding bands only. |
| Touching the tap or door handle after washing | Recontaminates clean hands immediately | Use paper towel on taps and door handles |

### Quick Reference Table

| Situation | Recommended Action | Escalate If |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| No soap at the handwash basin | Restock immediately from supplies cupboard. Do NOT substitute with washing-up liquid. | If soap stock is running low, inform Manager to reorder today |
| No paper towels at the basin | Replace from storage. Do NOT use tea towels or cloth. | If paper towel supply is below 1 day, inform Manager |
| Handwash basin blocked or not draining | Report to Manager immediately. Do NOT use the food prep sink instead. | Contact plumber if not resolved same day -- a non-functioning handwash basin is a closing condition for EHO |
| Staff member has cuts or sores on hands | Cuts must be covered with a blue waterproof plaster before handling food. Wash hands before and after applying plaster. | If the wound is infected (red, swollen, weeping), the staff member must not handle food -- reassign duties |
| Staff member refuses to wash hands or skips steps | Private conversation with the staff member. Explain the legal and safety reasons. | Persistent refusal is a disciplinary matter. Document it. |

### Further Learning

- Related blocks: B011 (Cleaning Schedule Protocol)
- Ask the Manager for a hands-on demonstration during induction
- Watch the Food Standards Agency handwashing video (available on their website)
- Practise the technique until it becomes automatic -- within 2 weeks of consistent practice, it becomes habit

---

## Common Risks

### Risk 1: Handwash Basin Used for Other Purposes

**Why it happens:** Staff rinse a cup, soak a cloth, or pour away leftover coffee in the handwash basin because it is the closest sink. This contaminates the basin with food residue and cleaning chemicals, making it unsuitable for handwashing.
**How to prevent:** Label every handwash basin clearly: "HANDWASHING ONLY -- No food, no equipment, no chemicals." If staff are using it because other sinks are inconvenient, consider the kitchen layout -- the handwash basin should be the easiest sink to reach.
**If it is already happening:** Clean the basin thoroughly. Repost the sign. Brief the team at the next shift change. If necessary, make the disciplinary consequence clear -- misusing a handwash basin is an EHO closing condition.

### Risk 2: Staff Wash Hands but Do Not Dry Them Properly

**Why it happens:** Paper towels run out and nobody restocks them. Or staff shake their hands dry in the air because they are in a hurry. Wet hands transfer bacteria far more effectively than dry hands -- up to 1000 times more.
**How to prevent:** Check paper towel stock at every shift start (add to opening checklist). Keep backup stock accessible near the basin. Brief staff: "Washing without drying is only half the job."
**If it is already happening:** Restock paper towels immediately. If you are routinely running out, increase your order quantity. Consider wall-mounted dispensers that make restocking visible.

### Risk 3: New Staff Not Shown the Correct Technique

**Why it happens:** Handwashing seems so basic that managers assume everyone knows how to do it. But studies consistently show that most people do not wash their hands correctly, even in food service environments. New starters bring habits from home -- which are usually insufficient for a commercial kitchen.
**How to prevent:** Include a physical handwashing demonstration in every induction, no matter how experienced the new starter is. It takes 2 minutes. Watch them wash their hands and correct technique in real time. Refer to this poster.
**If it is already happening:** Run a team refresher. Stand at the basin together. Demonstrate the full technique. Ask each team member to do it while you observe. Correct gently. This is not about embarrassment -- it is about building a shared standard.

---

## Customisation Notes

**This tool is a starting point, not a rulebook.**

**Independent / Specialty Cafe:** Print and laminate this poster for every handwash basin. If you have one basin behind the bar, one in the kitchen, and one near the staff toilet, that is three copies. Include it in your SFBB diary.
**Chain / Franchise:** Your brand may supply branded handwashing posters. Use this as a supplement or compare against the brand version to ensure all steps are covered.
**Kiosk / Mobile:** Space is limited, but handwashing is non-negotiable. Even a pop-up basin with a gravity-fed water container needs this poster nearby. In a mobile unit, hand sanitiser is NOT a substitute for handwashing -- it supplements but does not replace.
**Bakery Cafe:** Flour residue on hands can carry gluten to allergen-free areas. Emphasise thorough washing between handling flour-based products and gluten-free items.

**Making it yours:**
- Print on waterproof paper or laminate -- basin-side posters get wet
- Add your cafe logo to make it feel like yours, not a generic poster
- Include the trigger points list alongside the technique poster
- Check weekly that posters are still legible and in place

The structure matters more than the specific fields. Keep the structure, change the content.

---

## Connected Resources

**Other tools in this block:**
- T009-02 -- Handwashing Compliance Log (L1, checklist)
- T009-03 -- Handwashing Facility Check Log (L2, checklist)
- T009-04 -- Handwashing SOP Template (L2, SOP)
- T009-05 -- Quarterly Handwashing Audit Form (L3, audit)

**Related blocks:**
- B011

**Parent block:** B009 -- Handwashing Protocol
**Module:** M01: Food Safety & Hygiene Compliance
---

*Tool Version 1.0 -- OIS Cafe v2*
*Built for cafe operators who value clarity, consistency, and craft.*
